name: Bullas: Vía Verde and Carrascalejo Loop
name_original: Salto del Usero, Castillo de Don Fabio y Barranco de la Regidora — vuelta desde Bullas
description: Cycle the Bullas: Vía Verde and Carrascalejo Loop, a moderate 14.7-mile route through vineyards, pine forests, and railway heritage.
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around Bullas traverse a diverse landscape, featuring mountainous areas, cultivated plains with vineyards and almond trees, and serene river valleys. The region's topography offers varied terrain, from the gentle gradients of the Vía Verde del Noroeste to more challenging climbs within the Sierra Espuña Natural Park. Cyclists can explore river ecosystems, including the Mula River, and encounter unique geological formations. This area provides a rich backdrop for touring cyclists seeking varied experiences. Best touring cycling routes around Bullas The most popular touring cycling route is Bullas: Vía Verde and Carrascalejo Loop, a 14.7 miles (23.7 km) trail that takes 1 hour 44 minutes to complete. This moderate route combines sections of the Vía Verde with scenic countryside paths. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Greenway of the Northwest – Via Verde del Noroeste loop from Bullas, a moderate 27.9 miles (44.9 km) path. This route follows the historic railway line, offering views of the surrounding agricultural landscape and historical infrastructure. Local touring cyclists also love the Mula River Viaduct – La Luz Reservoir loop from Bullas, a 25.9 miles (41.7 km) trail leading through river valleys and past the La Luz Reservoir, often completed in about 3 hours 33 minutes. Touring cycling around Bullas is defined by diverse topography, extensive vineyards, and river valleys. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy greenways to more difficult mountainous routes. The routes in Bullas are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.6 stars from over 20 reviews. More than 300 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Bullas's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around Bullas?
A: There are over 35 touring cycling routes around Bullas, offering a wide variety of experiences for different skill levels and preferences.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes in Bullas?
A: Bullas offers a diverse landscape for touring cyclists. You can expect a mix of mountainous areas reaching around 1,000 meters, cultivated plains adorned with extensive vineyards and almond trees, and serene river valleys such as the River Mula. This varied topography provides a constantly changing backdrop for your rides.

Q: Are there easy touring cycling routes suitable for beginners or families in Bullas?
A: Yes, the region offers several easy routes. A prime example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1017160367/bullas-via-verde-and-carrascalejo-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bullas: Vía Verde and Carrascalejo Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which combines sections of the Vía Verde del Noroeste with scenic countryside paths. The Vía Verde del Noroeste itself is a converted railway line, generally flat and well-marked, making it ideal for families and less experienced riders.

Q: Can I cycle along the Vía Verde del Noroeste in Bullas?
A: Absolutely! Bullas is an integral part of the Vía Verde del Noroeste, a scenic rails-to-trails path. You can explore routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10321052" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Greenway of the Northwest – Via Verde del Noroeste loop from Bullas&lt;/a&gt;, which follows this historic railway line, offering views of the agricultural landscape and historical infrastructure. The segment between Bullas and Cehegín is particularly noted for its beauty.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes around Bullas?
A: Yes, many routes around Bullas are designed as loops. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1017160367/bullas-via-verde-and-carrascalejo-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bullas: Vía Verde and Carrascalejo Loop&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10321052" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Greenway of the Northwest – Via Verde del Noroeste loop from Bullas&lt;/a&gt; are popular circular options that allow you to start and end in the same location.

Q: Are there routes that pass by the Usero Waterfall?
A: Yes, you can cycle to the stunning Usero Waterfall (Salto del Usero) on the River Mula. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/19382163" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Usero Waterfall – Salto del Usero loop from Bullas&lt;/a&gt; is a challenging route that will take you past this beautiful geological formation. You can also find more information about the waterfall itself on its highlight page: &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4127769" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Usero Waterfall&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What are some notable landmarks or points of interest I can see while cycling in Bullas?
A: While cycling in Bullas, you can encounter various points of interest. Along the Vía Verde del Noroeste, you'll see historical railway infrastructure, tunnels, and viaducts. Near Cehegín, you might pass the ruins of the ancient Roman-Visigothic city "Deitana Begastri." The town of Bullas itself has impressive 20th-century buildings like Casa Melgares. Don't miss the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3226429" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tunnel Between Cehegín and Caravaca&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4137013" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vía Verde del Noroeste: Bullas to Cehegín&lt;/a&gt; highlight.

Q: Are there routes that explore the wine region of Bullas?
A: Yes, Bullas is renowned for its D.O. (Denominación de Origen) Bullas wines. Many cycling routes traverse extensive vineyards, offering a unique cultural and sensory experience. You'll find yourself cycling through the heart of this viticultural landscape, especially on routes that explore the cultivated plains around the town.

Q: When is the best time of year to go touring cycling in Bullas?
A: The best time to go touring cycling in Bullas is generally during spring and autumn when temperatures are milder. While cycling is possible year-round, it's recommended to avoid peak sun hours in summer due to high temperatures, and always carry sufficient water, especially on longer routes.

Q: What do other cyclists say about touring cycling in Bullas?
A: The touring cycling routes in Bullas are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.6 stars from over 29 reviews. Cyclists often praise the diverse topography, the extensive vineyards, and the serene river valleys that define the area, offering options for various ability levels.

Q: Is it possible to bring my dog on touring cycling routes in Bullas?
A: While specific regulations for dogs on all routes may vary, the Vía Verde del Noroeste is generally considered dog-friendly, making it a good option for cycling with your canine companion. Always ensure your dog is on a leash and that you carry water for both of you, especially during warmer months.

Q: Are there more challenging touring cycling routes in the Bullas area?
A: Yes, for those seeking a challenge, the region offers more difficult mountainous routes. The nearby Sierra Espuña Natural Park, for example, features routes with significant climbs, such as the Collado Bermejo, which ascends over 1,300 meters, rewarding cyclists with panoramic views. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31403842" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mula River Viaduct – La Luz Reservoir loop from Bullas&lt;/a&gt; is also a difficult route with substantial elevation gain.
tips:
Salto del Usero is a beautiful natural space located in the municipality of Bullas, in the Region of Murcia. This place is known for its impressive waterfall that falls through a limestone rock, forming an open dome and a pool of turquoise water.

To get to Salto del Usero, you need to take a short walk of approximately 1.5 km from the parking lot. During the high season, such as summer and holidays, it is necessary to make a reservation in advance to limit capacity and preserve the environment.

It is an ideal place to enjoy nature, go hiking and, during times of less affluence, enjoy a swim in its fresh waters. 😊

Located in the Northwest region, the municipality of Bullas offers various points of interest, such as the Parish Church of Nuestra Señora del Rosario (declared an Asset of Cultural Interest) and the Salto del Usero, ideal for bathing in summer, located on the outskirts of the location.

Located about 6 km. To the north of Bullas, on a farm and bordering on the west with the regional road from Bullas to La Copa, we find the Castle of Don Fabio, also known as El Palacete de la Fuente de la Higuera. It is a magnificent palace that is currently in a great state of abandonment.

faqs.question: Is the Bullas: Vía Verde and Carrascalejo Loop suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Vía Verde del Noroeste, which forms a significant part of this route, is generally considered easy with minimal gradients. It's built on a disused railway line, making it highly accessible for touring cyclists of all experience levels. Bullas is the highest point on the Vía Verde, so you'll experience gradual descents or climbs depending on your direction.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the Bullas: Vía Verde and Carrascalejo Loop?
faqs.answer: The terrain is varied and generally well-maintained. You can expect a mix of compacted soil, crushed gravel, and some asphalt sections. This makes it suitable for hybrid, gravel, and mountain bikes. The gentle gradients are a hallmark of Vía Verdes, as they follow old railway lines.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the route?
faqs.answer: The loop offers several interesting sights. You'll pass through picturesque landscapes of green pine forests, vineyards, and almond trees. Notable highlights include the beautiful Salto del Usero waterfall, the historic Castillo de Don Fabio, and the Parish Church of Our Lady of the Rosary in Bullas. The Carrascalejo area is also known for its Aleppo pines and vineyards, and the venerated Cristo del Carrascalejo.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the Vía Verde and Carrascalejo Loop?
faqs.answer: While specific parking for this exact loop isn't detailed, Bullas is a key access point for the Vía Verde del Noroeste. You can typically find parking within Bullas itself, especially near the Vía Verde access points or the former train station, which often serve as trailheads for cyclists.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle the Bullas: Vía Verde and Carrascalejo Loop?
faqs.answer: The Region of Murcia generally enjoys a mild climate. Spring (March to May) and Autumn (September to November) are ideal, offering pleasant temperatures for cycling and beautiful scenery, especially with the vineyards and almond trees. Summers can be very hot, and winters can be cooler, but often still suitable for cycling on sunny days.

faqs.question: Is the Bullas: Vía Verde and Carrascalejo Loop dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Generally, Vía Verdes in Spain are dog-friendly, allowing leashed dogs. However, it's always recommended to keep your dog on a leash, especially in populated areas or near wildlife, and to carry bags to clean up after them. Be mindful of local regulations in Bullas and the surrounding areas.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: No, you do not need a permit or have to pay an entrance fee to cycle the Vía Verde del Noroeste or the local roads that make up the Bullas: Vía Verde and Carrascalejo Loop. These are public access routes.

faqs.question: Are there facilities like cafes or accommodation along the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, Bullas itself offers various cafes, restaurants, and accommodation options. Along the Vía Verde del Noroeste, several old train stations have been rehabilitated into hostels (albergues) and rest areas, providing convenient stops for refreshments and potential overnight stays. The route passes through towns rich in history and culture, offering opportunities to explore local amenities.

faqs.question: Does the route intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Bullas: Vía Verde and Carrascalejo Loop incorporates sections of several other official trails. Notably, it uses parts of the Vía Verde del Noroeste, the Variante Asomadilla - Carrascalejo, and the Camino del Apóstol. Other intersecting paths include Transmurciana 16 and 17, Sendero del Romero, Camino de la Cruz del Bajo Guadalentín, and Sendero de la Atalaya.

faqs.question: Is this an official, named cycling trail?
faqs.answer: The "Bullas: Vía Verde and Carrascalejo Loop" is not formally recognized as a single named trail. Instead, it's a route that touring cyclists can create by leveraging sections of the well-established Vía Verde del Noroeste, which passes directly through Bullas, and local roads around the Carrascalejo area. This allows for a flexible and scenic loop incorporating these points of interest.

faqs.question: How long does it take to cycle the Bullas: Vía Verde and Carrascalejo Loop?
faqs.answer: The route is approximately 23.7 kilometers (14.7 miles) long. For a touring cyclist, it typically takes around 1 hour and 45 minutes to complete, depending on your pace and how often you stop to enjoy the scenery or visit points of interest.

waypoints.name: Parish Church of Our Lady of the Rosary, Bullas
